extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property G(opted_out implies not sent) requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true forces sent=false.

The flaw is transition t5: on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks>=6): sent:=true. This transition is explicitly enabled precisely when opted_out is true, and it sets sent:=true — directly producing a state where opted_out=true AND sent=true. Even though t3 (send_attempt if opted_out: sent:=false) also matches when opted_out is true and ticks>=6, t5 is an explicitly listed enabled transition in that condition, so there exists an execution in which it fires and yields the violating state.

Concrete reachable counterexample (8 events, within the 10-event bound):
1. optout            -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0   (t1)
2. tick              -> ticks=1, sent=false                   (t4)
3. tick              -> ticks=2, sent=false                   (t4)
4. tick              -> ticks=3, sent=false                   (t4)
5. tick              -> ticks=4, sent=false                   (t4)
6. tick              -> ticks=5, sent=false                   (t4)
7. tick              -> ticks=6, sent=false                   (t4)
8. send_attempt      -> t5 enabled (opted_out=true and ticks>=6): sent:=true

Final state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=6. Here opted_out is true but sent is also true, violating opted_out implies not sent. Clinically, this means an opted-out patient can still be sent a message once the tick counter saturates at 6 — exactly the unsafe behavior the property forbids.
